coredata.dc:title: A universal sugar-blowing approach to synthesize fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots for detection of Hg(II)
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Sen
Zhang, Yaqing
Gao, Shang
Fei, Teng
Zhang, Tong
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs) have been considered as promising materials for fluorescent sensing applications. However, preparation of CDs-based materials by a universal approach with high product yield is highly required. Herein, a novel sugar-blowing approach was reported to facilely synthesize nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) by directly heating the mixture of glucose and dicyandiamide (DCDA) in the absence of any solvents. As a typical blowing agent to trigger sugar-blowing process, DCDA also serves as an ideal nitrogen dopant for N-CDs. For fluorescent sensing applications, a remarkable fluorescence quenching at 441 nm of N-CDs dispersion was observed after introduction of Hg(II). Consequently, as-synthesized N-CDs behaves as a highly sensitive fluorescent probe for Hg(II) detection with low detection limit of 50 nM and good selectivity. More importantly, our proposed approach is universal for synthesize fluorescent N-CDs from a wide of sugar precursors (glucose, fructose, maltose and lactose) and blowing agents (DCDA, melamine, urea, hexamethylenetetramine). The strategy demonstrated here provides insights into the rational design and synthesis of CDs-based functional materials for large-scale applications in various fields.

(a) TEM image, (b) XRD pattern, (c) FT-IR spectrum and (d) solid state 13C NMR spectrum of N-CDs thus obtained. Insets of Fig. 2a shows HR-TEM image of a single nanoparticle and particle size distribution of N-CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, (b) high resolution C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s XPS spectra of N-CDs thus obtained.
A schematic illustration for formation of N-CDs by sugar-blowing approach.

PL emission spectra of N-CDs dispersion in the absence (black line) and presence (red line) of 50 µM Hg(II). Inset shows a scheme to illustrate the fluorescent detection of Hg(II) by N-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The PL spectra of N-CDs dispersion in the presence of different Hg(II) concentrations (from top to bottom: 0, 0.05, 0.1, 1, 5, 10, 30 and 50 µM), (b) the dependence of F/F 0 on the concentrations of Hg(II) within the range of 0–50 µM. (excitation: 360 nm; F 0 and F are N-CDs fluorescence intensities at 441 nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+ ions, respectively).

The difference in PL intensities of N-CDs dispersion between the blank and solutions containing different metal ions (excitation: 360 nm; the concentration of metal ions is 50 µM; F 0 and F are N-CDs fluorescence intensities at 441 nm in the absence and presence of ions, respectively).

head.ce:title: A universal sugar-blowing approach to synthesize fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots for detection of Hg(II)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Integrated Optoelectronics, College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Integrated Optoelectronics
College of Electronic Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Integrated Optoelectronics, College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs) have been considered as promising materials for fluorescent sensing applications. However, preparation of CDs-based materials by a universal approach with high product yield is highly required. Herein, a novel sugar-blowing approach was reported to facilely synthesize nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) by directly heating the mixture of glucose and dicyandiamide (DCDA) in the absence of any solvents. As a typical blowing agent to trigger sugar-blowing process, DCDA also serves as an ideal nitrogen dopant for N-CDs. For fluorescent sensing applications, a remarkable fluorescence quenching at 441 nm of N-CDs dispersion was observed after introduction of Hg(II). Consequently, as-synthesized N-CDs behaves as a highly sensitive fluorescent probe for Hg(II) detection with low detection limit of 50 nM and good selectivity. More importantly, our proposed approach is universal for synthesize fluorescent N-CDs from a wide of sugar precursors (glucose, fructose, maltose and lactose) and blowing agents (DCDA, melamine, urea, hexamethylenetetramine). The strategy demonstrated here provides insights into the rational design and synthesis of CDs-based functional materials for large-scale applications in various fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Hg(II) has been considered as a typical dangerous heavy metal pollutant in water environment, and Hg(II) could accumulate in organisms causing irreversible damage to the central nervous and endocrine system [1]. Therefore, it is highly required to develop high-performance detection methods for determination of trace Hg(II) with high sensitivity and selectivity. Up to now, researchers have successfully developed several technologies for Hg(II) analyses. Among these methods, fluorescent sensing strategy has been proved as a promising method for high-performance detection of Hg(II) [2,3]. Particularly, much attention has been paid to fabrication of Hg(II) sensors based on fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs), which exhibit excellent sensing performances, compared with other fluorescent nanomaterials [4]. Unfortunately, the previously reported approaches for CDs possess obvious shortcomings of complexion synthesis conditions and low product-yields.

Generally, fluorescent CDs are the carbon-based nanoparticles with size below 10 nm, possessing excellent advantages including unique fluorescence properties, good physicochemical stability, low toxicity, and so on [5,6]. As a result, fluorescent CDs have garnered considerable research interest for various applications during the past few years [7]. Since the serendipitous discovery of fluorescent CDs in 2004 [4], CDs-based materials have revealed excellent properties in the fields of fluorescent sensors [8], optoelectronic devices [9], photocatalysts [10], fluorescent agent for optical imaging [11] and electrocatalysts [12]. Although the applications of CDs in these realms were anticipated, the crucial challenge to push CDs to practical applications is development of scalable and effective synthesis strategies.

For the preparation of fluorescent CDs, the cutting of macroscopic carbon materials into small size nanoparticles/nanodots has been proved as an efficient method, and fluorescent CDs have been successfully synthesized by conventional top-down approach via chemical/physical treatment methods derived from various carbon-based materials [13–18]. However, there are still plenty of shortcomings limiting these approaches for use in preparation of CDs, including harsh synthetic process, low product yields and poor fluorescent properties. To overcome these disadvantages, great effort has been devoted to developing new strategies for fluorescent CDs from chemicals or biomasses. Previously, we have reported the preparation of nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) by hydrothermal treatment of grass for Cu(II) detection [19]. Additionally, hydrothermal synthesis method is widely used for preparation of CDs using several carbon-containing molecules as precursors, including biomasses [20–24], organic molecules [25–32]. During the formation of CDs, the polymerization of molecular precursors with each other into nanoparticles/nanodots comprised of C-C bonds is generally believed to be the fundamental principle. Consequently, the CDs-based materials were also synthesized by pyrolysis of carbon-containing materials [33–35]. Additionally, some special synthesis methods have also been developed for preparation of CDs, such as microwave irradiation [36], heating in hot edible oil [37], laser irradiation [38], ultrasound [39], reflux with acid [40]. Despite the progress has been made, low products yields and harsh synthesis conditions would largely hinder the practical application of CDs-based materials. Therefore, development of novel synthesis approaches for CDs becomes an urgent need to keep pace with their potential applications.

Most recently, Wang et al. have reported a sugar-blowing approach to grow three-dimensional (3D) graphene with high power densities for supercapacitors [41]. Consequently, such sugar-blowing method has been used to synthesize strutted graphene [42], graphene-like carbon nanosheets [43], porous cobalt phosphide/nitrogen-doped carbon nanostructures [44], 3D graphene-based on graphene oxide [45,46]. Previously, we have also synthesized mesoporous MgO nanosheets for electrochemical detection of lead(II) by the sugar-blowing approach using MgNO3 as blowing agent [47]. There is no doubt that the polymerization of sugar precursors into sugar-derived polymers, such as melanoidin and its analogs, contributes to the formation of carbon-based materials [41,47]. During the sugar-blowing approach, no any solvents were involved for the formation of carbon-based materials at wild conditions, leading to the obvious advantages of simple operation, high effectivity. As expected, if we could carefully regulate the synthesis conditions to decrease the particle size below 10 nm, it is capable of producing CDs-based materials on large-scale by the sugar-blowing approach. However, to date, there has been no report about preparation of fluorescent CDs by sugar-blowing approach, to the best of our knowledge.

Inspired by famous “blown sugar” in food technology, we demonstrate such sugar-blowing approach as a novel and universal strategy to synthesize fluorescent N-CDs. Unlike conventional sugar-blowing approach, dicyandiamide (DCDA) was used as blowing agent instead of frequently used NH4Cl, which not only serves as blowing agent to trigger sugar-blowing process, but also acts as nitrogen dopant for formation of N-CDs. Most importantly, our proposed sugar-blowing approach for N-CDs is a high-throughput and universal strategy using various sugar precursors (glucose, fructose, maltose lactose) and nitrogen-containing chemicals as blowing agents, including DCDA, melamine, urea and hexamethylenetetramine (HMT). Additionally, N-CDs thus obtained were used as fluorescent sensing platform for determination of Hg(II) with low detection limit and high selectivity. We believe that the present work is of significant importance for rational deign and synthesis of fluorescent CDs-based materials.

Glucose, fructose, maltose, lactose, chitosan, DCDA, melamine, urea, HMT, NH4Cl, o-phenylenediamine (oPD), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA), AgNO3, CaCl2, CdCl2, CoCl2, CuCl2, FeCl3, Hg(NO3)2, MaCl2, MnCl2, NiCl2, Pb(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 were purchased from Beijing Chemical Corp. (Beijing China). All chemicals were used without any further purification. The water used throughout all experiments was purified through a Millipore system. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) with the pH value of 7.0 was prepared by mixing stock solutions of NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4. Metal ion solutions were prepared by dissolving calculated metal ion salt solids into water directly.

The N-CDs were prepared by the sugar-blowing approach. In a typical experiment, 1 g of glucose and 0.75 g of DCDA were added into a glassy bottle with no cover. The bottle was transferred into an oven and heated at 180 °C for 20 min. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, the solid products were added into 500 mL of water, followed by sonication for 10 min. Then, N-CDs dispersion was obtained after removal of carbon materials with large particle size by vacuum filtration with the aid of microporous filter membrane (the particle size of 35 µm). N-CDs solid samples were obtained by removal of water in N-CDs dispersion by rotational evaporation, followed by freezing drying treatment.

In comparison, the N-CDs samples were also synthesized by addition of different content of DCDA into glucose, where the masses of DCDA were selected as 0.25 g, 0.50 g, 0.75 g and 1.0 g. The samples were also prepared by using various sugar precursors (fructose, maltose, lactose, chitosan) and blowing agents (melamine, urea, HMT, NH4Cl, oPD, EDTA), where the masses of sugar precursors and blowing agents were selected as 1.0 g and 0.75 g, respectively. The detailed synthesis conditions for these samples were shown in Table S1.

The X-Ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was performed on a Rigaku D/MAX 2550 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed on an ESCALABMK II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained from a UV-2450 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescent (PL) spectra were performed on a RF-5301PC spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurement was performed in a HITACHI H-8100 electron microscopy (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum was carried out on a WQF-510AFTIR spectrometer using air as the reference. The solid-state magic-angle spinning nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) experiment was carried out by using a Bruker AVANCE III 400 WB spectrometer. Zeta potential measurement was performed on a Nano-ZS Zetzsozer ZEN3600 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK).

The detection of Hg(II) ions was performed at room temperature in PBS (0.2 M, pH of 7.0) buffer solution. The N-CDs dispersion was prepared by adding 30 mg of N-CDs into 40 mL of water, followed by slight sonication for 5 min. In a typical run of Hg(II) detection, 20 µL of N-CDs dispersion was added into 1 mL of PBS buffer, followed by addition of a calculated amount of Hg(II) ions solution. The PL spectra were recorded after reaction for 30 min at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials preparation, characterizations, and experimental optimization
ce:section.ce:para: In bottom-up strategies for preparation of CDs, the difference between carbon precursors and CDs is the chemical composition and structure for carbon element. In other words, the prerequisites for formation of CDs is transferring carbon-containing bonds into ordered CC/CC bonds, along with the change of morphology and structure. As one of important precursors for carbon-based materials, sugars have been proved as promising candidates for various carbon materials. However, it is still required to carefully control the synthesis conditions for formation of small size CDs. Inspired by the sugar-blowing process, a modified sugar-blowing approach has been developed for preparation of N-CDs. A white solid powder was obtained after mixing glucose and DCDA in the absence of any solvents (Fig. 1 a, left), which conversed into reddish brown color solid with huge volume expansion after heating at 180 °C for 20 min (Fig. 1a, right), indicating the formation of sugar-blowing phenomenon. It is well known that glucose molted into liquid at 180 °C and DCDA also decomposed at this temperature [48,49]. Thus, the change of morphology and structure was attributed to the interaction between glucose and DCDA at 180 °C. In order to investigate the influence of sugar-blowing on the samples structure, the structure and properties of as-obtained samples was examined in detail. A uniform aqueous dispersion was obtained by dissolving as-synthesized solid into water, indicating the good dispersing ability of final products. The UV–vis spectrum of this dispersion reveals a strong absorption peak centered at 216 nm along with broad absorption to 350 nm (Fig. 1b, magenta line), which is different from the absorption spectrum of glucose solution, indicating the formation of newly carbon-based materials. Notably, this dispersion shows a strong PL emission peak at 441 nm, when it was excited at 360 nm (Fig. 1b, blue line). By contrast, the solid samples thus obtained exhibit no any fluorescent property under UV light irradiation (data not shown). The dispersion possessing PL property was further confirmed by the photographs. Normally, the dispersion exhibits a light yellow color (Fig. 1c), similar with other CDs aqueous dispersion. However, a brilliant blue color was observed by irradiating the dispersion at 365 nm (Fig. 1d). The effect of excitation wavelengths on fluorescent property of N-CDs was investigated, where the excitation wavelength was increased from 320 to 520 nm. As shown in Fig. S1a, the PL spectra of such dispersion gradually change from violet to yellowish-green, where PL emission peak moves to the longer wavelength and intensity gradually decreases. These results are attributed to characteristic of fluorescent CDs. The corresponding PL spectra after normalization also reveal this transformation (Fig. S1b). According to our previous work that N-doped carbon materials were obtained by pyrolysis of glucose and DCDA [48], the above observations suggest that N-CDs have been successfully prepared by the sugar-blowing approach. Similar with other fluorescent CDs derived from glucose, fluorescent properties of N-CDs could also be attributed to the optical selection with decreasing nanoparticles size (quantum effect), special emissive traps on the N-CDs surface, or another mechanism altogether [28]. Additionally, the purification of CDs is very important for the synthesis and applications of CDs. In order to overcome the disadvantages of dialysis method, the samples were prepared with relatively long reaction time, leading to the formation of nanoparticles or aggregations from sugar avoiding formation of products with small particle size or molecular level products. Generally, the purpose for purification of CDs is the removal of ultrasmall nanoparticles, molecular products or large nanoparticles with no PL property, where the above impurity could be adsorbed by N-CDs and subsequently interaction each other, leading to low quantum yield, poor stability of N-CDs dispersion, as well as poor sensing performances for detection of Hg(II). As shown in our work, the N-CDs thus obtained exhibit high quantum yield, good stability for aqueous dispersion and well sensing performances for Hg(II) detection. Thus, the conventional purification is avoided in the present work. But if the conventional prettification was used, better fluorescent property and sensing performances may be obtained, which will be examined in our future work.

The microstructure of as-synthesized N-CDs was characterized by TEM technique, and TEM image of N-CDs was shown in Fig. 2 a. The N-CDs are comprised of numerous monodispersed nanoparticles, further confirming the good dispersing ability of N-CDs. The N-CDs thus obtained display uniform particle size distribution with the particle size of 2–4 nm, obtained by statistical analysis from 50 nanoparticles (inset of Fig. 2a). The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of a single nanoparticle further confirming the formation of nanodots (Fig. 2a, inset). Unlike to amorphous carbon materials, discernible lattice fringes were observed on N-CDs, and the spacing of such fringes is 0.21 nm associated with (1 0 0) plane of graphene, indicating the formation graphitic N-CDs [50]. The XRD pattern of N-CDs suggests a broad diffraction peak centered at 22.8° (Fig. 2b). This peak is different from the diffraction peak corresponding to (0 0 2) plane for graphitic carbon, indicating formation of amorphous carbon phase [51,52]. The loss of diffraction peak attributed to graphitic carbon may be attributed to the relatively small particle size of N-CDs. The surface chemical composition of N-CDs was further examined by FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2c). The N-CDs exhibit broad absorption bands at 3000–3500 cm−1 attributed to stretching vibrations of OH and NH, indicating the formation of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of N-CDs [53]. Unlike conventional fluorescent CDs, two absorption peaks at 2204 cm−1 and 2163 cm−1 associated with CN bonds were observed, attributed to the formation of terminal cyano groups derived from DCDA [54]. Additionally, the observation of four peaks at 1642 cm−1, 1567 cm−1, 1508 cm−1 and 1079 cm−1 indicates the existence of stretching vibration of CC in benzene ring [55], CN bonds [56], NH bonds [57] and symmetric stretching vibrations of COC [58]. The peaks at 1477–1230 cm−1 suggest the formation of CO and CN bonds [59]. The chemical structure of carbon-containing bonds in N-CDs was further examined by solid state 13C NMR spectrum, and the corresponding spectrum was shown in Fig. 2d. Notably, several peaks attributed to various carbon-containing groups were observed, which are similar with the spectra of carbon materials derived from glucose, further confirming the formation of carbon-based materials from glucose [60]. The relatively high content of sp2 hybridized carbons in 13C NMR spectrum provides the evidence to confirm the formation of graphitic carbons by sugar-blowing approach.

Furthermore, Fig. 3 a shows the survey XPS spectrum of N-CDs, suggesting that N-CDs are comprised of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements with compositions of (C, 59.61%), (N, 21.74%) and (O, 18.65). Other two peaks for silicon element were attributed to the glassy substrate used for XPS test. The relatively high nitrogen-containing groups were obtained for N-CDs prepared by the sugar-blowing approach proposed in this work, which is attributed to the low synthesis temperature and high efficiency for introduction of nitrogen dopant by DCDA. In the high resolution XPS of C1s region (Fig. 3b), the peaks at 284.6, 286.0, 286.6 and 288.2 eV are associated with CC, CN, CO and CO bonds, agreement with the results of FT-IR and 13C NMR, indicating the formation of N-doped carbon materials [61]. Moreover, four characteristic peaks at 398.6, 399.8, 400.5 and 401.4 eV were observed in N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3c), assigned to pyridinic-N, amide/imine, pyridonic-N and graphitic-N bonds, respectively [62]. As shown in Fig. 3d, the O1s XPS spectrum consists of three peaks at 531.8, 532.5 and 533.9 eV, assigned to COO, CO, CO bonds, respectively [47]. All these observations indicate that the fluorescent N-CDs have been successfully synthesized by sugar-blowing approach.

As discussed above for sugar-blowing process, glucose molecules converted into N-CDs by the polymerization between them with the aid of DCDA, where numerous O-containing and N-containing groups were formed on the surface and the N-CDs exhibit excellent dispersing ability in water. Fig. S2 shows the Zeta potential of N-CDs dispersion obtained by direct dissolving the N-CDs into water, revealing that the Zeta potential of N-CDs dispersion is about −8.3 mV, suggesting the existence of carboxylic acid on the surface, which is agreement with the results of XPS and 13C NMR.

The formation of N-CDs is derived from the sugar-blowing phenomenon by heating the mixture of glucose and DCDA, and thus the effect of ratio of glucose to DCDA on the formation of fluorescent N-CDs was examined. The heating of glucose in the absence of DCDA results in translucent solid with light yellow color (Fig. S3A), which is attributed to the melting of glucose at 180 °C and subsequently cooling at room temperature. After dissolving the solid into water, the corresponding PL emission spectrum displays no obvious fluorescent property under UV irradiation at 360 nm (Fig. S4a), and UV–vis spectrum reveals no absorption from 200 nm to 800 nm (Fig. S4b). When increasing DCDA from 0.25 g to 1.0 g in sugar-blowing process, huge volume expansion accompanied by the color change from white to black were obtained (Fig. S3B-S3E), indicating the triggering sugar-blowing process. This result indicates that the observation of sugar-blowing phenomenon is attributed to synergistic effect between glucose and DCDA. By contrast, a white powder similar with DCDA was observed after heat treatment of DCDA in the absence of glucose (Fig. S3F), which could be attributed to the high melting point (207 °C). As expected, no PL emission spectrum was observed for the corresponding aqueous dispersion (Fig. S4a). All the samples obtained by heat treatment of mixture containing glucose and DCDA exhibit obvious fluorescent properties under UV light irradiation (Fig. S4a). Among all these samples, N-CDs-0.75 prepared from 1 g of glucose and 0.75 g of DCDA exhibits the strongest PL spectrum at the same conditions.

Moreover, the effect of reaction time on fluorescent property of N-CDs was also examined. Fig. S5a shows the photographs of solid products obtained by heat treatment of 1.0 g of glucose and 0.75 g of DCDA at 180 °C with the reaction time from 0 min to 20 min. After heating for 5 min, no obvious change of solid mixture was observed compared with the mixture of glucose and DCDA. By increasing the reaction time to 10 min, an orange transparent liquid was observed, indicating the triggering sugar-blowing process. Further increasing reaction time to 15 min, the orange product changed into reddish brown solid. A further heating treatment for 20 min, the volume of the solid was rapidly expanding along with numerous bubbles. Additionally, the PL property of the dispersion obtained by dissolving the solid products into water was examined, as shown in Fig. S5b. It is seen that the dispersions with the reaction time from 0 min to 10 min exhibit no fluorescent property. And the strongest PL spectrum of the dispersion with reaction time of 20 min was observed. However, it is hard to completely dissolve the solid products into water with further increasing the reaction time longer than 20 min. Moreover, Fig. S6 shows the emission and excitation bandwidth of N-CDs dispersion, indicating that bandwidths for emission and excitation spectra are 2.81 eV and 3.13 eV, respectively. It is seen that both emission and excitation bandwidth display typical asymmetrical spectra, which are different from the ideal fluorescent spectra with symmetrical shape. This could be attributed to the spectral dispersion of the lasers used for fluorescent property test, although monochromator was used to select the excitation and emission light, especially the selected emission light with relatively low bandwidth. The quantum yield of N-CDs was examined using quinine sulfate as standard according to previous method (see detailed information in Supporting information), and the quantum yield of N-CDs is calculated to be 36.6%.

Notably, our proposed sugar-blowing approach is a highly effective method for preparation of N-CDs on a large scale. It is found that the product yield of N-CDs is calculated to be 61.3% (after purification, 1.0725 g of N-CDs was obtained by sugar-blowing approach derived from 1 g of glucose and 0.75 g of DCDA). Fig. S7a shows the photograph of the samples obtained with increasing the precursors amount upto 5 times compared to the typical samples prepared in Experimental section (5 g of glucose and 3.75 g of DCDA). To examine the fluorescent property of this N-CDs, the PL emission spectrum of the N-CDs dispersion under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm was shown in Fig. S7b. Notably, there is no obvious difference in PL emission spectra between these N-CDs in Fig. S7 and N-CDs-0.75, indicating that the present method could be used for preparation of N-CDs on a large scale. The reason could be attributed to the formation of N-CDs derived from the polymerization of sugar at wild condition in the absence of no any solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sugar-blowing approach as a universal method for preparation of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As the blowing agent, DCDA is an essential component for the formation sugar-blowing. It is deduced that nitrogen-containing molecules derived from decomposition of DCDA play an important role in triggering sugar-blowing progress. To test this hypothesis, other organic and inorganic nitrogen-containing chemicals were tried to be used as blowing agent for the preparation of N-CDs, including melamine, urea, HMT, NH4Cl, oPD and EDTA, and corresponding chemical structures are shown in Fig. S8. The photographs of the solid products after heat treatment process were shown in Fig. S9a. After dissolving solid products into water, Fig. S9b-d show the PL emission spectra excited at 360 nm and photographs under UV irradiation at 365 nm. As we known, DCDA is a promising precursor for preparation of graphitic carbon nitride by the decomposition and polymerization of DCDA. Similar with DCDA, other nitrogen-containing organic molecules easily decomposed into nitrogen-containing groups were used instead of DCDA, including melamine, urea and HMT. As shown in Fig. S9a, dark brown solid products with huge volume expansion were observed for samples in the presence of melamine or HMT, indicating the formation of sugar-blowing progress. A dark brown solid product with small volume expansion was also observed in the presence of urea. The PL spectra of aqueous dispersion for these samples reveal obvious PL emission peaks at about 440 nm excited at 360 nm (Fig. S9b and S9c). The formation of fluorescent N-CDs by the sugar-blowing approach was also confirmed by photographs with blue color under UV irradiation at 365 nm (Fig. S9d). The previous reports have shown that NH4Cl is a promising blowing agent for preparation of graphene-based materials by sugar-blowing progress [41,42]. In the present work, the heat treatment of glucose and NH4Cl results in black solid products with huge volume expansion. However, the aqueous dispersion exhibits no obvious PL spectrum excited at 360 nm and light blue color under UV irradiation at 365 nm, indicating poor fluorescence property. As a typical nitrogen-containing molecule with good stability, oPD was used as additive instead of DCDA. Although a black solid product with slightly volume expansion was obtained, the corresponding aqueous dispersion exhibits no fluorescent property. Another nitrogen-containing molecule, EDTA was also used for preparation of N-CDs. Although the sugar-blowing was observed, the product shows no obvious fluorescent property (Fig. S9b-S9d). All these observations indicate that N-CDs have been successfully prepared by the sugar-blowing approach using DCDA, melamine, urea and HMT as blowing agents. These observations further confirm that it is necessary to decompose blowing agents into nitrogen-containing molecules in sugar-blowing process for the formation of N-CDs.

One the other hand, the formation of fluorescent N-CDs by the sugar-blowing progress is also attributed to the unique structure of glucose. Thus, the effect of sugar precursors on the formation of N-CDs was also examined, and four other organic molecules were used instead of glucose, including fructose, maltose, lactose and chitosan. Fig. S10 shows the molecular structures of these organic molecules. As shown in Fig. S11a, obvious sugar-blowing phenomenon was observed by using fructose, maltose and lactose as precursors. By contrast, no obvious color change was observed by heating the mixture of chitosan and DCDA. The fluorescent property of these samples was analyzed by PL spectra and photographs. As shown in Fig. S11b and S11c, the dispersions of samples prepared by fructose, maltose and lactose exhibit strong PL spectra, similar with the PL spectrum of N-CDs from glucose. The corresponding photographs irradiated at 365 nm indicate that fluorescent N-CDs could be also obtained by using fructose, maltose and lactose as precursors. However, it is hard to prepare fluorescent N-CDs using chitosan as precursor (Fig. S11d). All these observations suggest that the monosaccharides without in the polymers are highly required for the formation of sugar-blowing.

As discussed above, the formation of N-CDs is attributed to the unique sugar-blowing phenomenon from sugar and nitrogen-containing blowing agent. This is the first time to represent the preparation of fluorescent CDs-based materials by sugar-blowing approach, although this approach has been used to synthesize graphitic carbon materials [41–45]. Fig. 4 shows a schematic illustration of the formation of N-CDs by sugar-blowing approach. By subjecting to heating at 180 °C, solid glucose powders were converted into molten liquid due to the relatively low melting point of glucose (146 °C). Although the molten point of DCDA is 207 °C, DCDA molecules were decomposed into nitrogen-containing molecules and gases in the presence of molten glucose at 180 °C, such as NH3, N2 and other cyanides [63,64]. The decomposition of DCDA is based on the following two aspects. (I) The observation of sugar-blowing morphology leading to the formation of N-CDs. (II) The preparation of graphitic carbon nitrides from DCDA by heat treatment process. Subsequently, the NH3, and other cyanides serve as typical alkaline catalysts, and catalyze the polymerization of molten syrup, leading to formation of diverse sugar-derived polymers, such as melanoidin and its analogues. Similar with conventional sugar-blowing process, the releasing of gases from DCDA blows molten syrup into bubbles. Additionally, the polymerization of molten syrup also results in the releasing gases. As a result, the huge volume expansion of the mixture was observed. Under the synergistic effect of glucose and DCDA, a dark brown solid product with huge volume expansion was observed. During the sugar-blowing process, the glucose was polymerized into isolated nanoparticles, and nitrogen atom derived from DCDA was in situ doped into the matrix of nanoparticles, leading to formation of N-CDs. In order to avoid formation of carbon-based materials with large particle size, or graphene-based materials, the heat treatment process was selected at low temperature with short heating time. According to this reaction mechanism, the present sugar-blowing approach for preparation of fluorescent N-CDs also could be achieved by numerous sugar precursors and nitrogen-containing blowing agents. Indeed, N-CDs were successfully obtained by using various sugar precursors (fructose, maltose, lactose) and nitrogen-containing blowing agents (melamine, urea, HMT, NH4Cl), as shown in Fig. S9 and S11.

The reason for the observation of PL band at 441 nm in N-CDs is also very important for applications of N-CDs. Recently, Messina and co-authors have investigated the relationship between the microstructures of CDs and the PL properties [65,66]. Similar with their conclusion, the observation of PL in N-CDs could be attributed to the formation of fluorescent carbon particles with small particle size and the existence of chromophores at molecule level on the surface of carbon nanoparticles.

Compared with the previously reported methods for preparation of N-CDs, our proposed approach exhibits several advantages shown as follow. (I) Wild synthesis conditions. The N-CDs were obtained by simple heat treatment of mixture containing glucose and DCDA at relatively low temperature with short time (180 °C, 20 min). By contrast, the conventional bottom-up methods required heating the precursors at higher temperature (200–300 °C). (II) High product yields. The polymerization reaction was formed in a wild condition in the absence of any solvents, leading to little quality lost. With further increasing the mass of precursors, N-CDs could be obtained on a large-scale. (III) Synthesis method of universality. Because the sugar-blowing process could be achieved with different sugar precursors and blowing agents, and thus our proposed approach is universal. (IV) In situ doping nitrogen atom. The formation of fluorescent nanodots and introduction of nitrogen dopant were obtained at the same times, where no any other operations of surface modification or doping were required.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of Hg(II) by N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the present work, abundant nitrogen/oxygen-containing groups are formed on the surface of N-CDs. The previous reports have shown that these groups are considered as surface active sites for adsorption of metal ions. Therefore, fluorescent CDs act as a kind of promising fluorescent probes for fabrication of optical metal ion sensors. To investigate the sensing application, we examined the feasibility of such N-CDs obtained by the sugar-blowing approach for Hg(II) detection by fluorescent technology. As shown above, a strong PL emission spectrum at 441 nm was observed for N-CDs dispersion in the absence of Hg(II) (the excitation wavelength is 360 nm). By contrast, when 50 µM Hg(II) was introduced into N-CDs dispersion, fluorescence in intensity of N-CDs dispersion was tremendously decreased, suggesting that the Hg(II) can effectively quench the fluorescence of N-CDs (Fig. 5 ). This observation was illustrated by the scheme in inset of Fig. 5, and the possible fluorescence quench mechanism could be attributed to the interaction between N-CDs and Hg(II). It is well known that the extranuclear electronic structure of Hg(II) is [Xe]5d10, where the 5d orbital was completely filled by electrons, leading to no coordination bonds formed by 5d orbital. However, the lone pair electrons in N or O atoms on the surface of N-CDs could also transfer into the 6 s or 6p orbitals of Hg(II) due to small differences in energies of atomic orbital between 5d, 6 s, and 6p orbitals, resulting in the formation of coordination bonds. As a result, electron or energy transfer carried out between N-CDs and Hg(II) after UV light irradiating aqueous dispersion containing N-CDs and Hg(II), and substantial fluorescence quenching was subsequently obtained. As discussed in previous publication [67], the PL bleaching phenomenon is also observed for N-CDs obtained by the sugar-blowing approach in this work, which is same to the CDs prepared from organic molecules. Fortunately, the whole detection process for detection of Hg(II) performed in the absence of UV light, besides the PL test (only several seconds). Thus, the PL bleaching has affected on sensing performances very slightly. Indeed, series of CDs have been used for detection of metal ions during the past few years. Thus, the detection mechanism for Hg(II) should be attributed to the PL quenching of N-CDs by Hg(II), as shown in Fig. 5.

Additionally, Fig. S12 shows the PL spectra of N-CDs dispersion containing 50 µM Hg(II) with irradiation from 0 to 30 min. Notably, the fluorescence intensity decreases sharply within the first 5 min, and gradually decreases with further prolonging heating time upto 30 min. Additionally, the optimization conditions for Hg(II) detection were also examined. As shown in Fig. S13, the better detection efficiency was obtained with increasing the volume of N-CDs dispersion from 5 µL to 20 µL, and no obvious change for detection efficiency was observed with further increasing the volume of N-CDs dispersion to 30 µL. Fig. S14 shows the differences in PL intensity of 50 µM Hg(II) in the presence of 20 µL of N-CDs dispersion with increasing the pH value from 6.0 to 8.0, indicating that pH values have no effect on detection efficiency.

The tremendous fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Hg(II) indicates that N-CDs thus obtained are promising fluorescent probe for fabrication of optical Hg(II) sensor. To investigate the relationship between the PL intensity of N-CDs and Hg(II) concentrations, the PL spectra of N-CDs dispersion in the presence of various Hg(II) concentrations was show in Fig. 6 a. The PL spectra of N-CDs dispersion gradually decreased with increasing Hg(II) concentrations ranging from 50 nM to 50 µM, which could be attributed to the formation of more coordination bonds by N or O atoms and Hg(II). The corresponding relationship between the F/F 0 and Hg(II) concentrations are shown in Fig. 6b, where F 0 and F are the intensities of PL emission spectra at 441 nm for N-CDs dispersion in the absence and presence of Hg(II). Notably, the detection limit of present Hg(II) sensor is estimated to be 50 nM.

The selectivity is another very important factor for Hg(II) sensors because the Hg(II) was always coexisting with other metal ions, and the PL intensity changes of N-CDs dispersion in the absence of metal ions and in the presence of various metal ions with the concentration of 50 µM are shown in Fig. 7 , where the metal ions include Ag+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+. Interestingly, the biggest fluorescence quenching of N-CDs dispersion was obtained upon addition of Hg(II), compared with other metal ions, indicating that the N-CDs-based Hg(II) sensors exhibit good selectivity. It is also found that the addition of Ag+, Cu2+ and Ni2+ results in slightly decreasing fluorescence intensity. The different fluorescence quenching among all these metal ions could be attributed to the interaction between N-CDs and metal ions. To explain this observation, the extranuclear electronic structures, elecreonegativity, and ionic radium (sixfold-coordination) of all these metal ions were shown in Table S2. As alkaline-earth metal ions, it is difficult to form coordination bonds between N/O and Ca2+/Mg2+ because of their small ionic radius and high charge number. Similarly, small ionic radius and high charge number also result in struggling to form coordination bonds for Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, and Zn2+. Although Cu2+ and Ni2+ also exhibit small ionic radius, the presence of empty d orbitals leadings to easily accept electrons from N/O-containing groups. It is seen that Ag+ and Pb2+ also have large ionic radiuses. The weak fluorescence quench for Ag+ may be attributed to its low ion polarization, compared with Hg2+. And no fluorescence quenching of Pb2+ is attributed to the large electronegativity. All these observations indicate that the N-CDs-based sensor exhibits good selectivity for determination of Hg(II). The interference test indicates that the other metal ions has affected on the detection efficiency for detection of Hg(II), but not very seriously, as shown in Fig. S15.

The sensing performances of present Hg(II) sensor based on N-CDs were further compared with the recently reported fluorescent Hg(II) based on various CDs (Table S3). Notably, our proposed Hg(II) sensor displays the low detection of 50 nM, which is much lower than that of newly reported Hg(II) sensors based on gold and nitrogen co-dopedcarbonquantum dots(118 nM) [68], and carbon dots (201 nM) [69], N,S-codoped CDs (83 nM) [70] and B,N-CDs (2.8 µM) [71]. Although the detection our proposed Hg(II) sensors is higher than that of N,S-GQDs (0.14 nM) [72], pyrophosphate-modified CDs (2 nM) [73], and CD nanohybrids (0.14 nM) [74], N-CDs-based Hg(II) exhibits wider detection range (0.5–50 µM) than that of pyrophosphate-modified CDs (01–1.4 µM) [73], and CD nanohybrids (0.0006–0.8 µM) [74].

Additionally, the detection limit of the present Hg(II) sensor is still higher than the maximum concentration (5 nM) recommended by World Health Organization (WHO). Therefore, it is highly required to improve the sensitivity of the sensor for the practical applications in the future. The improvement of sensitivity of N-CDs-based Hg(II) sensor could be obtained by the following aspects. (I) Construction of surface actives of N-CDs, ensuring the selective adsorption of Hg(II), except other coexisting metal ions. (II) Tuning the composition of N-CDs, improving the electron transfer efficiency between N-CDs and Hg(II). Based on the above discussions, the Hg(II) sensors with high-performance could be obtained in our future work.

In summary, we have demonstrated a universal sugar-blowing approach for preparation of N-CDs using various sugar precursors (fructose, maltose, lactose) and nitrogen-containing blowing agents (melamine, urea, HMT, NH4Cl). The formation of N-CDs attributed to unique chemical reactions between sugar and blowing agents under heating, where the polymerization of sugar into nanopartilces with small particles size and the nitrogen atoms derived from decomposition of blowing agents in situ doped into carbon matrix. The results indicated that N-CDs can act as a promising fluorescent probe for fabrication of optical Hg(II) sensor, leading to low detection limit of 50 nM. This study reveals a universal approach for preparation of N-CDs, paving new way for synthesis of CDs-based functional materials for their potential applications. This work also represents a promising alternative for fluorescent probe for other sensing applications.
